## Topic
Classical homological algebra: the **acyclic-resolution comparison theorem** for
right-derived functors. Precisely: if `0 → A → J⁰ → J¹ → ⋯` is a resolution of an
object `A` in an abelian category by objects `Jⁿ` that are **right-`F`-acyclic** for an
additive (left-exact) functor `F` (i.e. the higher right-derived functors
`(R^k F)(Jⁿ)` vanish for all `k ≥ 1`), then the derived functor of `F` is computed by the
resolution: `(R^n F)(A) ≅ Hⁿ(F(J•))`. Also want the **definition of an `F`-acyclic
object** and the standard **dimension-shifting** proof (truncate the resolution into short
exact sequences `0 → A → J⁰ → Z¹ → 0`, use the long exact sequence of derived functors,
and induct).

this abstract theorem (it is the homological-algebra heart of an acyclic-cover / Cartan–Leray
route to computing higher direct images). I need a real, openly-available source to quote
**verbatim** for: (a) the definition of an `F`-acyclic object, and (b) the statement +
proof of "an `F`-acyclic resolution computes the derived functor". I will paste the
verbatim text into `% SOURCE QUOTE:` / `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` comments, so I need the exact
original wording, notation, and the section/tag/theorem number.

  resolutions" and the definition of "right acyclic object" / "F-acyclic". Search the
  downloaded TeX for `acyclic`, `right derived functor`, `\delta`-functor.
  - Tarball of all TeX: https://github.com/stacks/stacks-project/archive/refs/heads/master.tar.gz

- **Secondary (textbook):** Weibel, *An Introduction to Homological Algebra* (CUP 1994),

  If an openly-available PDF exists, fetch it; if it is paywalled, do NOT fabricate — rely on

48	
49	# Reference Retriever
50	
51	You fetch the **original source files** — papers, books, online mathematical content — from the web and save them under the project's `references/` directory, then register each with a lightweight pointer file. You exist so the plan agent and blueprint-writers can read and quote the genuine source verbatim, rather than relying on a paraphrase (which drifts) or the dispatcher's memory (which fabricates).
52	
53	The single most important rule: **download the real file. Do not substitute a summary for it.** A paraphrase introduces exactly the errors (wrong lemma numbers, wrong tags, subtly mis-stated hypotheses) that this subagent exists to eliminate.
54	
55	## Your Job
56	
57	Your directive names one or more topics, mathematical questions, or specific sources (by arXiv ID, DOI, title + author, or URL). For each, you:
58	
59	1. Locate the source on the web (`WebSearch` + `WebFetch` for discovery).
60	2. **Download the original file(s)** with `curl`/`wget` via `Bash` into `references/`. Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when it is openly available, AND keep the PDF too.
61	3. **Verify** the download is the real document, not an HTML paywall/error page saved under a `.pdf` name.
62	4. Write a lightweight **pointer file** `references/<slug>.md`: citation, local path(s), a contents/section map (table of contents), and caveats. **No paraphrased content.**
63	5. Register the new entry in `references/summary.md`.
64	6. Report back to the dispatcher with a one-line status + the report path.
65	
66	You do **not** hallucinate citations or content. If a source cannot be obtained through any legitimate open channel, you report that honestly. The dispatcher prefers an honest "not found" over a fabricated or paraphrased stand-in every time.
67	
68	## Directive Format
69	
70	```markdown
71	# Reference Retriever Directive
72	
73	## Slug
74	<slug>
75	
76	## Topic
77	<one or two sentences naming the mathematical area + the specific question>
78	
79	## What the dispatcher will use this for
80	<one paragraph: which chapter / strategy decision the source will inform, so I can prioritise which sections to map and which formats to chase>
81	
82	## Seeds (optional)
83	- arXiv: 1234.5678
84	- DOI: 10.xxxx/yyyy
85	- Title + author: "Some Paper" by Smith, year
86	- URL: https://...
87	- Search query: "<terms the dispatcher recommends>"
88	
89	(If no seeds are given, I search for the topic on arXiv + Google + Stacks Project + nLab as appropriate to the area.)
90	
91	## Out of scope
92	<sources or angles the dispatcher does NOT want — e.g. "no philosophy-of-math sources", "skip historical surveys, want technical content only">
93	
94	## Contents-map depth expected
95	- shallow: top-level table of contents only
96	- medium: section/chapter map with the relevant section(s) flagged + page numbers (default)
97	- deep: also map the specific theorems/lemmas the dispatcher named, with their exact location (section + page) inside the downloaded file — but still NO paraphrase of their content
98	```
99	
100	If the directive omits "Contents-map depth expected", default to **medium**.
101	
102	## What you do
103	
104	1. **Read your directive completely.**
105	
106	2. **Locate the source.**
107	   - If seeds are given, resolve them first.
108	   - Otherwise, `WebSearch` with focused queries derived from the topic. Prefer:
109	     - arXiv for recent technical papers.
110	     - Stacks Project (https://stacks.math.columbia.edu) for algebraic geometry — its TeX source is open on GitHub.
111	     - nLab (https://ncatlab.org) for category-theoretic content.
112	     - Open-access textbooks and lecture notes when the topic is foundational.
113	
114	3. **Download the original file(s)** with `Bash`. Prefer machine-readable source and keep the PDF too:
115	
116	   - **arXiv**: fetch BOTH the LaTeX e-print and the PDF.
117	     ```bash
118	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.tar.gz https://arxiv.org/e-print/<id>
119	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf     https://arxiv.org/pdf/<id>
120	     ```
121	     If the e-print is a single `.tex`, name it `references/<slug>.tex` instead. You may unpack the tarball (`tar xzf`) into `references/<slug>-src/` if it helps map the contents — but keep the original archive too.
122	
123	   - **Stacks Project**: fetch the relevant `.tex` chapter verbatim from GitHub raw, so lemma/tag statements are exact (this is the fix for the paraphrase/tag-typo failure mode):
124	     ```bash
125	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>-algebra.tex \
126	       https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stacks/stacks-project/master/algebra.tex
127	     ```
128	     Record the exact tags the dispatcher needs and confirm each tag's statement against the fetched `.tex`.
129	
130	   - **Journal / book with an open form**: download the open PDF (author homepage, institutional repository, numdam, Project Euclid open, publisher open-access, etc.).
131	
132	   - **General**:
133	     ```bash
134	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf "<url>"
135	     ```
136	     `-f` makes curl fail on HTTP errors instead of silently saving an error page.
137	
138	4. **Verify the download is real**, not a paywall/login/error page wearing a `.pdf` extension:
139	   ```bash
140	   ls -l references/<slug>.pdf
141	   file references/<slug>.pdf      # expect "PDF document", not "HTML document"
142	   ```
143	   A near-empty file, an HTML MIME type, or a "log in to continue" body all mean the fetch FAILED — delete the bad file (`rm references/<slug>.pdf`) and treat that source as not obtained. **Never** rename or keep an unverified download.
144	
145	5. **Write the pointer file** `references/<slug>.md` (format below) in BOTH the success and failure cases:
146	   - **On success:** it points at the real downloaded file(s) and records the exact URL each was retrieved from. It does NOT reproduce the source's content.
147	   - **On failure** (nothing could be downloaded and verified): write the pointer anyway, recording the citation, every URL/channel you tried, and why each failed (paywall, 404, HTML-only, etc.). **Do NOT write the source's mathematical content into it** — a failure pointer carries the attempt log, never a paraphrase or recollection of what the source "probably says."
148	
149	6. **Register the entry** in `references/summary.md`. Append (do not delete existing entries) a one-line index entry referencing the slug.
150	
151	7. **Verify the files are on disk** (`ls -l references/<slug>.*`) before reporting back.
152	
153	## Acquiring paywalled / hard-to-find sources
154	
155	Many sources that look paywalled have a legitimate open copy. Before giving up, try, in order:
156	
157	- **arXiv** for the same title/authors — journal papers very often have an arXiv preprint with identical statements.
158	- **The author's personal / institutional homepage** — search `"<title>" <author> pdf` and `<author> publications`.
159	- **Open repositories**: numdam.org (French journals, seminars — this is where Grothendieck's FGA and Séminaire Bourbaki live), Project Euclid open content, institutional repositories, course pages.
160	- **`filetype:pdf` web searches**: `WebSearch` for `"<exact title>" filetype:pdf`. Some hits open a PDF directly — fetch and verify it.
161	- **Open TeX source**: Stacks Project (GitHub), many lecture notes and books publish LaTeX source openly.
162	
163	**Do NOT** use shadow-library / piracy sites (e.g. Sci-Hub, Library Genesis). Stick to legitimately open copies. If, after these steps, only a paywalled copy exists, fetch whatever open metadata you can (official TOC, abstract) into the pointer file's contents map, mark the source `PAYWALLED — no open copy located`, and say so plainly in your report. Do not paraphrase the body to compensate.
164	
165	## Pointer file format
166	
167	`references/<slug>.md` is an index card, not a summary. Keep it short.
168	
169	### Success — file(s) downloaded and verified
170	
171	```markdown
172	# <Title>
173	
174	## Citation
175	<author(s)>, "<title>", <venue / publisher>, <year>. <DOI / arXiv ID / URL>.
176	
177	## Slug
178	<slug>
179	
180	## Retrieval status
181	RETRIEVED — <date>
182	
183	## Local source files
184	- references/<slug>.pdf — PDF, VERIFIED via `file` — retrieved from <successful URL>
185	- references/<slug>.tex — LaTeX source, VERIFIED via `file` — retrieved from <successful URL>   ← if obtained
186	(List every file you saved and the EXACT URL it came from. These successful links are
187	the most important field — a later planner uses them to re-fetch or double-check.)
188	
189	## Why this source
190	<2-3 sentences: which Archon chapter / strategy decision will use this. Copy from the directive's "What the dispatcher will use this for".>
191	
192	## Contents map
193	<A table of contents / section map pointing INTO the downloaded file, so a planner knows where to look. Use the source's own numbering. NO paraphrase of the mathematics — just locations.>
194	
195	- Ch./§ <n> <title> — p.<page>
196	- Ch./§ <n> <title> — p.<page>   ← relevant to this directive
197	- (deep depth only) Theorem/Lemma <number> — §<n>, p.<page>
198	
199	## Caveats
200	<Things the dispatcher should know: paywalled-but-open-copy-found, unusual conventions, known errata, contested results, edition differences. If none, write "None".>
201	
202	## Quality / provenance
203	<one or two sentences: is this the definitive reference or one of several? Where exactly did the file come from, and how confident are you it is the authoritative version?>
204	```
205	
206	### Failure — nothing could be downloaded and verified
207	
208	Still write the pointer, but it is an **attempt log**, not content. A future planner reading it must immediately see that there is no local source to quote and what was already tried (so they don't burn an iteration repeating it).
209	
210	```markdown
211	# <Title>
212	
213	## Citation
214	<author(s)>, "<title>", <venue / publisher>, <year>. <DOI / arXiv ID / URL>.
215	
216	## Slug
217	<slug>
218	
219	## Retrieval status
220	NOT_RETRIEVED — <date>. No local source file exists. DO NOT cite this as `(read from …)`.
221	
222	## Sources tried
223	- <url 1> — <what happened: 404 / paywall / HTML-only login page / corrupt file>
224	- <url 2> — <…>
225	- <search query used> — <no open copy among results>
226	
227	## Why it matters / what to do next
228	<which chapter/decision needed this, and the dispatcher's options: find another route, pick a different source, or treat the dependent block as gated on retrieval>
229	
230	<!-- NO contents map, NO abstract, NO recalled statements. There is nothing verified to record. -->
231	```
232	
233	## `references/summary.md` registration
234	
235	Append one row to the `## File inventory` table. The template has three columns: `File`, `Description`, `How to read (confirmed working)`. Make the retrieval outcome visible in the index itself, so a planner skimming `summary.md` sees at a glance which sources actually have a local file AND which tool they should use to ingest each one:
236	
237	```markdown
238	| [`<slug>.md`](./<slug>.md) → `<slug>.pdf` / `<slug>.tex` | <one-line topic note> | `Read` (with `pages: "1-5"` if long); fallback `pdftotext <slug>.pdf -` |
239	| [`<slug>.md`](./<slug>.md) → ⚠ NOT RETRIEVED (<short reason>) | <topic note> | n/a — no local file |
240	```
241	
242	For the `How to read` column, record what *actually worked* during your verification step (you opened the file to verify it isn't an HTML-paywall stub — write down the command you used). `Read` for PDFs assumes ``poppler-utils`` is installed (``archon setup`` handles it); if `Read` errors with a missing-`pdftoppm` message, record the exact `pdftotext` (or other) fallback you used. The column is a living log — later agents who ingest the same file should overwrite the entry with a better entry if they find one.
243	
244	If `references/summary.md` doesn't exist, create it with the standard header + the inventory table before appending.
245	
246	## Rules
247	
248	### What you CAN do
249	- Use `WebSearch` and `WebFetch` to discover and inspect sources.
250	- Use `Bash` to download, unpack, and verify source files. **Use `curl` — it is present on every supported platform, so no extra install is needed.** `wget` is an optional fallback only if `curl` is somehow missing (`command -v curl || command -v wget`); never assume `wget` exists. `tar`, `file`, and `ls` are likewise standard.
251	- Write downloaded files and the pointer `.md` under `references/`.
252	- Append to `references/summary.md`.
253	
254	### What you MUST do
255	- **Download the real file and verify it.** A pointer file is only valid once its named source file exists on disk and `file` confirms it is the real document.
256	- **Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when openly available, and keep the PDF too.** TeX gives exact statements; the PDF is the universal fallback.
257	- **Cite exact section / page numbers** in the contents map. A map that just says "the paper discusses X" is useless.
258	- **Quote nothing in the pointer file.** Planners quote from the downloaded source, not from you. Your contents map only tells them where to look.
259	
260	### What you MUST NOT do
261	- **Do NOT fabricate or paraphrase the source's content.** No "I believe the paper proves…", no reconstructed proofs in the `.md`. You fetched the file or you didn't.
262	- **Do NOT use piracy / shadow-library sites.** Legitimate open copies only.
263	- **Do NOT delete or rewrite existing `references/` entries.** Only add new ones. If an existing entry is wrong, report it in "Notes for Dispatcher" — do not modify it.
264	- **Do NOT write outside `references/`.**
265	- **Do NOT spawn child subagents.** You are a leaf.
266	
267	## Report format
268	
269	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/reference-retriever-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
270	
271	```markdown
272	# Reference Retriever Report
273	
274	## Slug
275	<slug>
276	
277	## Status
278	<COMPLETE | NOT_FOUND | PARTIAL>
279	<NOT_FOUND: no legitimate open copy of any source could be downloaded.
280	PARTIAL: some sources/formats obtained, others not (e.g. PDF but no TeX, or paywalled).
281	COMPLETE: all directive items downloaded and verified.>
282	
283	## Sources fetched
284	
285	For each: title, URL(s) used, formats downloaded, verification result, pointer file written.
286	
287	- "Some Paper" — https://arxiv.org/abs/1234.5678 — downloaded `references/some-paper.pdf` (PDF, verified) + `references/some-paper.tar.gz` (TeX source, verified) — pointer `references/some-paper.md`.
288	- "Other Book Ch. 5" — paywalled, no open copy located — TOC-only pointer written, NO source file.
289	
290	## Index updates
291	- `references/summary.md` — appended <N> entries: `some-paper`, ...
292	
293	## Notes for Dispatcher
294	<- any source the directive named that turned out to be wrong / mislocated, with the corrected pointer
295	- any related source you noticed that the dispatcher might want next
296	- any pre-existing references/ entry you noticed was wrong or stale (DO NOT modify — report only)
297	- any source you could only obtain paywalled, so the dispatcher can decide whether to find another route>
298	```
299	
300	## Return value
301	
302	Your final assistant message:
303	
304	- One line: `<slug>: <status> — <N> sources downloaded (<formats>), <M> pointer files written`
305	- The path to your full report.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

5	## Priority Rule

10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.

22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

33

40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

Let us temporarily say that a partial functor is one that is
defined on a subcategory.

\begin{definition}
\label{definition-derived-functor}
In
Situation \ref{situation-classical}.
\begin{enumerate}
\item The {\it right derived functors of $F$} are the partial functors
$RF$ associated to cases (1) and (2) of
Situation \ref{situation-classical}.
\item The {\it left derived functors of $F$} are the partial functors
$LF$ associated to cases (3) and (4) of
Situation \ref{situation-classical}.
\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
{\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
{\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
if $A[0]$ computes $LF$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{definition}

\noindent
The following few lemmas give some criteria for the existence of
enough acyclics.

\begin{lemma}
\label{lemma-subcategory-left-resolution}
Let $\mathcal{A}$ be an abelian category. Let

\begin{lemma}
\label{lemma-F-acyclic}
Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
between abelian categories and assume
$RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
$F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
\item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
If $A$ is right acyclic for $F$, then $RF(A[0]) = F(A)[0]$ and in
particular $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and
$R^iF(A) = 0$ for $i \not = 0$. Conversely, if $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$
is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$ then
$F(A[0]) \to RF(A[0])$ is a quasi-isomorphism by
Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived} part (1)
and hence $A$ is acyclic. If $F$ is left exact then $F = R^0F$, see
Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived}.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
\label{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}
Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
between abelian categories and assume
$RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
of $\mathcal{A}$.
\begin{enumerate}
\item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
\item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
\item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
\end{enumerate}
In each of the three cases
$$
0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
$$
is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
By
Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}
we obtain a distinguished triangle
$(A[0], B[0], C[0], a, b, c)$ in $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
As $RF$ is an exact functor and since
$R^iF = 0$ for $i < 0$ and $R^0F = F$
(Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived})
we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
$$
0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots
$$
in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$. Thus the lemma follows from
the characterization of acyclic objects in
Lemma \ref{lemma-F-acyclic}.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
\label{lemma-right-derived-delta-functor}
Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
between abelian categories and assume
$RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere defined.
\begin{enumerate}
\item The functors $R^iF$, $i \geq 0$ come equipped with a canonical
structure of a $\delta$-functor from $\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$, see
Homology, Definition \ref{homology-definition-cohomological-delta-functor}.
\item If every object of $\mathcal{A}$ is a subobject of a right
acyclic object for $F$, then $\{R^iF, \delta\}_{i \geq 0}$ is a
universal $\delta$-functor, see
Homology, Definition \ref{homology-definition-universal-delta-functor}.
\end{enumerate}
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The functor $\mathcal{A} \to \text{Comp}^{+}(\mathcal{A})$,
$A \mapsto A[0]$ is exact. The functor
$\text{Comp}^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$
is a $\delta$-functor, see
Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}.
The functor $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is exact.
Finally, the functor $H^0 : D^{+}(\mathcal{B}) \to \mathcal{B}$
is a homological functor, see
Definition \ref{definition-unbounded-derived-category}.
Hence we get the structure of a $\delta$-functor from
Lemma \ref{lemma-compose-delta-functor-homological}
and
Lemma \ref{lemma-exact-compose-delta-functor}.
Part (2) follows from
Homology, Lemma \ref{homology-lemma-efface-implies-universal}
and the description of acyclics in
Lemma \ref{lemma-F-acyclic}.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Leray's acyclicity lemma]

5692: \begin{lemma}[Leray's acyclicity lemma]
5693: \label{lemma-leray-acyclicity}
5694: Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
5695: between abelian categories. Let $A^\bullet$ be a bounded below complex
5696: of right $F$-acyclic objects such that $RF$ is defined at
5697: $A^\bullet$\footnote{For example this holds if
5698: $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere defined.}.
5699: The canonical map
5700: $$
5701: F(A^\bullet) \longrightarrow RF(A^\bullet)
5702: $$
5703: is an isomorphism in $D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$, i.e., $A^\bullet$ computes
5704: $RF$.
5705: \end{lemma}
5706: 
5707: \begin{proof}
5708: Let $A^\bullet$ be a bounded complex of right $F$-acyclic objects.
5709: We claim that $RF$ is defined at $A^\bullet$ and that
5710: $F(A^\bullet) \to RF(A^\bullet)$ is an isomorphism in $D^+(\mathcal{B})$.
5711: Namely, it holds for complexes with at most one nonzero right $F$-acyclic
5712: object by Definition \ref{definition-derived-functor}.
5713: Next, suppose that
5714: $A^n = 0$ for $n \not \in [a, b]$. Using the ``stupid'' truncations we obtain
5715: a termwise split short exact sequence of complexes
5716: $$
5717: 0 \to \sigma_{\geq a + 1} A^\bullet \to A^\bullet \to
5718: \sigma_{\leq a} A^\bullet \to 0
5719: $$
5720: see
5721: Homology, Section \ref{homology-section-truncations}.
5722: Thus a distinguished triangle
5723: $(\sigma_{\geq a + 1} A^\bullet, A^\bullet, \sigma_{\leq a} A^\bullet)$.
5724: By induction hypothesis $RF$ is defined for the two outer complexes
5725: and these complexes compute $RF$. Then the same is true for the middle one
5726: by Lemma \ref{lemma-2-out-of-3-computes}.
5727: 
5728: \medskip\noindent
5729: Suppose that $A^\bullet$ is a bounded below complex of acyclic objects
5730: such that $RF$ is defined at $A^\bullet$.
5731: To show that $F(A^\bullet) \to RF(A^\bullet)$
5732: is an isomorphism in $D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$
5733: it suffices to show that $H^i(F(A^\bullet)) \to H^i(RF(A^\bullet))$
5734: is an isomorphism for
5735: all $i$. Pick $i$. Consider the termwise split short exact sequence of
5736: complexes
5737: $$
5738: 0 \to \sigma_{\geq i + 2} A^\bullet \to A^\bullet \to
5739: \sigma_{\leq i + 1} A^\bullet \to 0.
5740: $$
5741: Note that this induces a termwise split short exact sequence
5742: $$
5743: 0 \to \sigma_{\geq i + 2} F(A^\bullet) \to F(A^\bullet) \to
5744: \sigma_{\leq i + 1} F(A^\bullet) \to 0.
5745: $$
5746: Hence we get distinguished triangles
5747: $$
5748: (\sigma_{\geq i + 2} A^\bullet, A^\bullet,
5749: \sigma_{\leq i + 1} A^\bullet)
5750: \quad\text{and}\quad
5751: (\sigma_{\geq i + 2} F(A^\bullet), F(A^\bullet),
5752: \sigma_{\leq i + 1} F(A^\bullet))
5753: $$
5754: Since $RF$ is defined at $A^\bullet$ (by assumption)
5755: and at $\sigma_{\leq i + 1}A^\bullet$ (by the first paragraph)
5756: we see that $RF$ is defined at $\sigma_{\geq i + 2}A^\bullet$
5757: and we get a distinguished triangle
5758: $$
5759: (RF(\sigma_{\geq i + 2} A^\bullet), RF(A^\bullet),
5760: RF(\sigma_{\leq i + 1} A^\bullet))
5761: $$
5762: See Lemma \ref{lemma-2-out-of-3-defined}.
5763: Using these distinguished triangles we obtain a map of exact sequences
5764: $$
5765: \xymatrix{
5766: H^i(\sigma_{\geq i + 2} F(A^\bullet)) \ar[r] \ar[d] &
5767: H^i(F(A^\bullet)) \ar[r] \ar[d]^\alpha &
5768: H^i(\sigma_{\leq i + 1} F(A^\bullet)) \ar[r] \ar[d]^\beta &
5769: H^{i + 1}(\sigma_{\geq i + 2} F(A^\bullet)) \ar[d] \\
5770: H^i(RF(\sigma_{\geq i + 2} A^\bullet)) \ar[r] &
5771: H^i(RF(A^\bullet)) \ar[r] &
5772: H^i(RF(\sigma_{\leq i + 1} A^\bullet)) \ar[r] &
5773: H^{i + 1}(RF(\sigma_{\geq i + 2} A^\bullet))
5774: }
5775: $$
5776: By the results of the first paragraph the map $\beta$ is an isomorphism.
5777: By inspection the objects on the upper left and the upper right
5778: are zero. Hence to finish the proof it suffices to show that
5779: $H^i(RF(\sigma_{\geq i + 2} A^\bullet)) = 0$ and
5780: $H^{i + 1}(RF(\sigma_{\geq i + 2} A^\bullet)) = 0$.
5781: This follows immediately from
5782: Lemma \ref{lemma-negative-vanishing}.
5783: \end{proof}
5784:
